# Prompt 2: The check runner

Add the eval checks to /Users/sbigelow/Workspace/evals-in-ci. Two verifiers, mirroring the
essay's "two different verifiers, same lesson" — one schema check, one
property check. Keep it to two small files.

## checks/test_schema.py
Runs kubeconform against every YAML in fixtures/ with strict mode and
the resource.k8s.io/v1 schemas enabled. One pytest test, parametrized
per file. Add kubeconform to mise.toml as a tool; do not shell out to
an unpinned binary.

## checks/test_properties.py
Property checks kubeconform can't express — the "claims traceable to
source" tier. Parse fixtures with PyYAML and assert 3 properties, e.g.:
- every container sets resource requests and limits
- no image uses the :latest tag
- DRA ResourceClaimTemplate uses the GA resource.k8s.io/v1 request shape
Pick properties the vendored fixtures actually satisfy; the suite must
pass green. Add pyyaml to pyproject.

## Constraints
- pytest is the only runner. `mise run test` (or `uv run pytest`) is the
  whole interface — no wrapper scripts, no config beyond pyproject.
- Every assertion failure message must name the file and the violated
  property. A red check that doesn't say why is off-thesis.
- No helper modules, no abstractions. If a check needs a function, it
  lives in the same file.

## Commits
One commit per check file, plain messages. Confirm Entire.io captured
both sessions. Show me the passing pytest output.

addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - API usage, tool use, Anthropic SDK usage. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.

SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

# ADR-0008: DRA — declarative accelerator requests instead of Device Plugin
# extended-resource counting. GA since v1.34.
apiVersion: resource.k8s.io/v1
kind: DeviceClass
metadata:
  name: nvidia-gpu
spec:
  selectors:
    - cel:
        expression: 'device.driver == "gpu.nvidia.com"'
---
apiVersion: resource.k8s.io/v1
kind: ResourceClaimTemplate
metadata:
  name: gpu-slice-claim
  namespace: team-ml
spec:
  spec:
    devices:
      requests:
        # GA resource.k8s.io/v1: a request names a class via `exactly`
        # (vs `firstAvailable` for prioritized alternatives). deviceClassName
        # and count live under `exactly`, not directly on the request.
        - name: gpu
          exactly:
            deviceClassName: nvidia-gpu
            count: 1
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: inference-worker
  namespace: team-ml
spec:
  # Non-root, no privilege escalation, all capabilities dropped, seccomp on.
  # DRA injects the GPU device without needing extra pod privileges.
  securityContext:
    runAsNonRoot: true
    runAsUser: 1000
    seccompProfile:
      type: RuntimeDefault
  containers:
    - name: worker
      image: inference-worker:1.0
      resources:
        requests:
          cpu: "2"
          memory: "8Gi"
        limits:
          cpu: "4"
          memory: "16Gi"
        claims:
          - name: gpu
      securityContext:
        allowPrivilegeEscalation: false
        capabilities:
          drop: ["ALL"]
  resourceClaims:
    - name: gpu
      resourceClaimTemplateName: gpu-slice-claim

apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: otel-collector
  namespace: platform-observability
spec:
  replicas: 2
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: otel-collector
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: otel-collector
    spec:
      securityContext:
        runAsNonRoot: true
        seccompProfile:
          type: RuntimeDefault
      containers:
        - name: otel-collector
          # 'contrib' build — redaction processor lives here, not in core.
          # Pinned to a specific release for reproducibility (bump deliberately);
          # never :latest in a cluster deployment.
          image: otel/opentelemetry-collector-contrib:0.155.0
          args: ["--config=/etc/otel/collector.yaml"]
          env:
            - name: DEPLOY_ENV
              value: "prod"
          ports:
            - containerPort: 4317   # OTLP gRPC
            - containerPort: 4318   # OTLP HTTP
          resources:                 # ADR-0009
            requests:
              cpu: "250m"
              memory: "512Mi"
            limits:
              cpu: "1"
              memory: "1Gi"
          securityContext:
            allowPrivilegeEscalation: false
            readOnlyRootFilesystem: true   # collector writes nothing to the root FS with this config
            capabilities:
              drop: ["ALL"]
          volumeMounts:
            - name: config
              mountPath: /etc/otel
              readOnly: true
      volumes:
        - name: config
          configMap:
            name: otel-collector-config
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: otel-collector
  namespace: platform-observability
spec:
  selector:
    app: otel-collector
  ports:
    - name: otlp-grpc
      port: 4317
    - name: otlp-http
      port: 4318

# ADR-0003: Gateway API for traffic routing (replaces legacy Ingress for new work)
apiVersion: gateway.networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Gateway
metadata:
  name: primary-gateway
  namespace: platform-ingress
spec:
  gatewayClassName: envoy-gateway   # any maintained Gateway API controller
  listeners:
    - name: https
      protocol: HTTPS
      port: 443
      tls:
        mode: Terminate
        certificateRefs:
          - name: primary-gateway-tls
      # Default is `Same`, which would reject the tenant HTTPRoutes below (they
      # live in team-* namespaces, not platform-ingress). Allow routes from any
      # namespace carrying a `tenant` label so cross-namespace routes attach.
      allowedRoutes:
        namespaces:
          from: Selector
          selector:
            matchExpressions:
              - key: tenant
                operator: Exists
---
# Basic single-backend route: the default path for the host. The /payments
# path is owned by the weighted canary route in httproute-traffic-split.yaml —
# keep exactly one route per (hostname, path prefix) so Gateway API conflict
# resolution never has to pick a winner between them.
apiVersion: gateway.networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: HTTPRoute
metadata:
  name: example-api-route
  namespace: team-payments
spec:
  parentRefs:
    - name: primary-gateway
      namespace: platform-ingress
  hostnames:
    - "api.example.internal"
  rules:
    - matches:
        - path:
            type: PathPrefix
            value: /
      backendRefs:
        - name: storefront-svc
          port: 8080

17	    # containers are normally injected AFTER creation via the
18	    # pods/ephemeralcontainers subresource, which this policy does NOT match,
19	    # so `kubectl debug`-style ephemeral containers are NOT gated at admission
20	    # here. scripts/validate.py flags ephemeralContainers at CI time.

# ADR-0007: reject Pods pulling from unapproved registries. GA since v1.30.
apiVersion: admissionregistration.k8s.io/v1
kind: ValidatingAdmissionPolicy
metadata:
  name: restrict-image-registries
spec:
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups: [""]
        apiVersions: ["v1"]
        operations: ["CREATE", "UPDATE"]
        resources: ["pods"]
  validations:
    # This policy matches resources: ["pods"] — i.e. CREATE/UPDATE of the Pod
    # object. It checks images on containers, initContainers, and any
    # ephemeralContainers already present on that object. Note: ephemeral
    # containers are normally injected AFTER creation via the
    # pods/ephemeralcontainers subresource, which this policy does NOT match,
    # so `kubectl debug`-style ephemeral containers are NOT gated at admission
    # here. scripts/validate.py flags ephemeralContainers at CI time.
    # The init/ephemeral lists are optional on the spec, hence the has() guards.
    - expression: >
        object.spec.containers.all(c,
          c.image.startsWith("registry.internal.example.com/") ||
          c.image.startsWith("gcr.io/distroless/")
        ) &&
        (!has(object.spec.initContainers) ||
          object.spec.initContainers.all(c,
            c.image.startsWith("registry.internal.example.com/") ||
            c.image.startsWith("gcr.io/distroless/")
          )
        ) &&
        (!has(object.spec.ephemeralContainers) ||
          object.spec.ephemeralContainers.all(c,
            c.image.startsWith("registry.internal.example.com/") ||
            c.image.startsWith("gcr.io/distroless/")
          )
        )
      message: "All container images (containers, initContainers, ephemeralContainers) must come from an approved registry."
---
apiVersion: admissionregistration.k8s.io/v1
kind: ValidatingAdmissionPolicyBinding
metadata:
  name: restrict-image-registries-binding
spec:
  policyName: restrict-image-registries
  validationActions: [Deny]
  matchResources:
    namespaceSelector:
      matchExpressions:
        - key: tier
          operator: NotIn
          values: ["platform-system"]   # platform namespaces exempted

"""Schema check: every fixture must validate against its Kubernetes schema.

Runs kubeconform (pinned in mise.toml) in strict mode. Kubernetes 1.34.1
schemas cover the GA resource.k8s.io/v1 DRA types; the CRDs-catalog location
covers Gateway API, which is a CRD and absent from the core schemas.
"""

import shutil
import subprocess
from pathlib import Path

import pytest

FIXTURES = sorted((Path(__file__).resolve().parent.parent / "fixtures").glob("*.yaml"))

KUBECONFORM_ARGS = [
    "-strict",
    "-kubernetes-version", "1.34.1",
    "-schema-location", "default",
    "-schema-location",
    "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/datreeio/CRDs-catalog/main/{{.Group}}/{{.ResourceKind}}_{{.ResourceAPIVersion}}.json",
]


@pytest.mark.parametrize("fixture", FIXTURES, ids=lambda p: p.name)
def test_fixture_matches_schema(fixture: Path):
    kubeconform = shutil.which("kubeconform")
    assert kubeconform, (
        "kubeconform not on PATH — it is pinned in mise.toml; run `mise install`"
    )
    result = subprocess.run(
        [kubeconform, *KUBECONFORM_ARGS, str(fixture)],
        capture_output=True,
        text=True,
    )
    assert result.returncode == 0, (
        f"{fixture.name}: failed strict kubeconform schema validation:\n"
        f"{result.stdout}{result.stderr}"
    )

Done — both checks are in, committed separately, and the suite is green: **16 passed** via `mise run test`.

**[checks/test_schema.py](checks/test_schema.py)** — one parametrized test running kubeconform in `-strict` mode against each of the 4 fixtures. It uses Kubernetes 1.34.1 schemas (the version where `resource.k8s.io/v1` went GA, so the DRA types validate against real schemas) plus the datree CRDs-catalog location for Gateway API, which is a CRD and absent from core schemas. kubeconform is pinned to **0.8.0** in [mise.toml](mise.toml), and the test fails with a `run mise install` hint if the binary is missing rather than falling back to anything unpinned. I also added a `test` task so `mise run test` works.

**[checks/test_properties.py](checks/test_properties.py)** — three properties kubeconform can't express, each parametrized per fixture, parsing with PyYAML (added to the dev group):
1. Every container (including initContainers, in Pods and pod templates) sets `resources.requests` *and* `resources.limits`.
2. Every image pins an explicit tag — `:latest` fails, and so does an untagged image, since that defaults to latest.
3. Every `ResourceClaimTemplate` uses the GA `resource.k8s.io/v1` shape: device requests nest `deviceClassName` under `exactly`/`firstAvailable` rather than on the request itself.

Every assertion message names the fixture file and states the violated property, e.g. `otel-collector-deployment.yaml: Deployment/otel-collector containers[otel-collector] does not set resources.limits — every container must declare resource requests and limits`.

**Commits** — one per file, plain messages. Per your mid-task message I amended the first commit to drop the Co-Authored-By line before it was pushed anywhere, and the second was made without one:
- `be5aa8b checks: kubeconform schema validation for fixtures` (+ mise.toml, .gitignore, uv.lock)
- `8c85df0 checks: property checks on fixtures` (+ pyproject.toml, uv.lock)

**Entire.io** — confirmed: `entire status` shows both sessions (the scaffold session `4dc52f3` and this one `7ba5e51`), and `entire explain --commit` resolves each new commit to a checkpoint on `entire/checkpoints/v1` tied to this session's transcript.

One thing worth knowing: uv resolved the venv to Python 3.14.0 (it satisfies `requires-python >= 3.13`, but mise pins python 3.13). If you want the venv to match the mise pin exactly, adding `requires-python = ">=3.13,<3.14"` or a `.python-version` file would do it — left as-is since everything passes.

Here's an overview of everything accomplished in this project so far:

## evals-in-ci — Project Overview

**Goal:** A minimal working example of running evals as CI checks — two different verifiers over the same vendored Kubernetes manifests, demonstrating that schema validation and property checks are two tiers of the same lesson.

### Session 1 — Scaffold (commits `b650b71`, `abc777a`)
- Initialized the repo with mise (Python 3.13, uv), a uv-managed `pyproject.toml` with pytest in the dev group, a README stub, and Entire.io hooks for session capture.
- Vendored 4 Kubernetes manifests into [fixtures/](fixtures/) from `bigelow/kubernetes-2026` @ `3be18b5`, with provenance recorded in [PROVENANCE.md](fixtures/PROVENANCE.md): an OTel collector Deployment, a ValidatingAdmissionPolicy for image registries, a DRA DeviceClass/ResourceClaimTemplate/Pod, and a Gateway + HTTPRoute.

### Session 2 — The check runner (commits `be5aa8b`, `8c85df0`)

**Verifier 1: schema check** — [checks/test_schema.py](checks/test_schema.py)
- One pytest test, parametrized per fixture file, running **kubeconform 0.8.0** in `-strict` mode.
- Uses Kubernetes **1.34.1** schemas (where `resource.k8s.io/v1` DRA types went GA) plus the datree CRDs-catalog for Gateway API, which isn't in core schemas.
- kubeconform is pinned as a tool in [mise.toml](mise.toml) — no unpinned binaries; the test tells you to run `mise install` if it's missing.

**Verifier 2: property checks** — [checks/test_properties.py](checks/test_properties.py)
- Three properties kubeconform's schemas can't express, parsed with PyYAML, each parametrized per fixture:
  1. Every container (incl. initContainers) sets both `resources.requests` and `resources.limits`.

- Every assertion failure message names the fixture file and the violated property — a red check always says why.

**Interface:** `mise run test` (or `uv run pytest`) is the whole thing — no wrapper scripts, no config beyond pyproject. **Result: 16 passed** (4 schema × 1 test, 4 fixtures × 3 properties).

**Housekeeping:** Added a `.gitignore` and committed `uv.lock`; Entire.io confirmed capturing both sessions with checkpoints tied to each commit; commit messages are plain with no Co-Authored-By lines; test cache artifacts were cleaned up after the runs.

input.content:
# Evals in CI: a minimal working example

This repo runs evals as ordinary CI checks: two verifiers over the same four
vendored Kubernetes manifests, on every push. There is no framework and no
harness — just pytest, a pinned validator, and a GitHub Actions job small
enough to read in one screen. The point is that an eval is only as good as
what its checks can actually verify, so I built the two tiers side by side.

## The two tiers

The schema tier is [checks/test_schema.py](checks/test_schema.py). It runs
kubeconform in strict mode against every fixture, using the Kubernetes 1.34.1
schemas (where the `resource.k8s.io/v1` DRA types are GA) plus a CRD catalog
for Gateway API. This catches everything a schema can express: unknown
fields, wrong types, retired API versions.

The property tier is [checks/test_properties.py](checks/test_properties.py).
It parses the same fixtures with PyYAML and asserts three things a schema
cannot: every container declares resource requests and limits, every image
pins a real tag rather than `:latest`, and every ResourceClaimTemplate uses
the GA `resource.k8s.io/v1` request shape. When one of these fails, the
message names the file and the violated property — a red check that doesn't
say why is off-thesis.

## Provenance

The fixtures are vendored from
[bigelow/kubernetes-2026](https://github.com/bigelow/kubernetes-2026) at a
pinned commit, recorded file by file in
[fixtures/PROVENANCE.md](fixtures/PROVENANCE.md). Pinning is the point: a
claim about these manifests is evidence tied to an inspectable state, not to
whatever the source repo happens to contain today.

## Run it

    mise install
    mise run test

## Evidence trail

Every commit in this repo has an [Entire.io](https://entire.io) session
checkpoint recorded alongside it, so the work that built the verifier is
itself inspectable — you can trace each check back to the session that wrote
it, the same way the checks trace each fixture back to its source.

## Companion essay

The essay this repo accompanies:
[Evals as CI](https://bigelow.github.io/posts/evals-as-ci/).

## License

Apache 2.0 — see [LICENSE](LICENSE).

+harness — just pytest, a pinned validator, and a GitHub Actions job small
+enough to read in one screen. The point is that an eval is only as good as
+what its checks can actually verify, so I built the two tiers side by side.
+kubeconform in strict mode against every fixture, using the Kubernetes 1.34.1
+schemas (where the `resource.k8s.io/v1` DRA types are GA) plus a CRD catalog
+for Gateway API. This catches everything a schema can express: unknown
+It parses the same fixtures with PyYAML and asserts three things a schema
+cannot: every container declares resource requests and limits, every image
+pins a real tag rather than `:latest`, and every ResourceClaimTemplate uses
+the GA `resource.k8s.io/v1` request shape. When one of these fails, the
+message names the file and the violated property — a red check that doesn't
+pinned commit, recorded file by file in
+claim about these manifests is evidence tied to an inspectable state, not to
+whatever the source repo happens to contain today.
+Every commit in this repo has an [Entire.io](https://entire.io) session
+checkpoint recorded alongside it, so the work that built the verifier is
+itself inspectable — you can trace each check back to the session that wrote
+it, the same way the checks trace each fixture back to its source.

content.text:
Done — the repo is public and CI is green on GitHub.

- **Repo:** https://github.com/bigelow/evals-in-ci (description: "Evals as CI checks — a minimal working example")
- **Actions run:** https://github.com/bigelow/evals-in-ci/actions/runs/28623143373 — completed, success, in 14s. The CI log shows kubeconform 0.8.0 installing via mise and `16 passed in 1.30s`, matching the local run exactly.

What went in, as two plain commits:

**`8448c9b` — [.github/workflows/ci.yml](.github/workflows/ci.yml)** (18 lines): one workflow, one job, triggered on push and pull_request to main. Four steps — checkout, mise-action, `mise install`, `mise run test`. Both actions are pinned to full commit SHAs with the version tag in a comment (`jdx/mise-action` @ v4.2.0, `actions/checkout` @ v7.0.0), and mise-action's auto-install is turned off so the explicit `mise install` step is the one that runs. No caching, no matrix.

**`fdd85ed` — [README.md](README.md) + [LICENSE](LICENSE)**: the stub is replaced with 51 lines of first-person prose covering all seven sections — what this is, the two tiers (naming `checks/test_schema.py` and `checks/test_properties.py` and what each can and can't verify), provenance pointing to [fixtures/PROVENANCE.md](fixtures/PROVENANCE.md) with the pinning-is-the-point framing, the two-line run instructions, the Entire.io evidence-trail paragraph, the companion essay link, and Apache 2.0. LICENSE is the full Apache 2.0 text.

One thing worth knowing: the push also published the `entire/checkpoints/v1` branch (the entire hook pushes it automatically), so the session checkpoints the README describes are actually inspectable on the public repo — the evidence-trail claim is backed. Test cache artifacts from the local verification run were cleaned up, and the working tree is clean.
